commands.description: Specifies the Compose file format version for backward compatibility. (Deprecated in the modern Compose Specification but still widely seen in older projects).
commands.mistake: Using a version number that does not match your Docker Engine capabilities, or quoting the version improperly causing parsing errors.

commands.description: The root key under which you define all the containerized components of your multi-container application.
commands.example:
services:
  web:
    image: nginx:alpine
  db:
    image: postgres:15-alpine
commands.mistake: Nesting service definitions at the wrong indentation level (they must be indented under 'services:').

commands.description: Declares named volumes at the top level to share persistent storage across multiple services or persist data beyond container lifecycle.
commands.example:
services:
  db:
    image: postgres:15-alpine
    volumes:
      - db_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data

volumes:
  db_data:
commands.mistake: Declaring a volume on a service without also defining it in the top-level 'volumes:' section (unless using raw host paths).

commands.description: Declares custom networks at the top level to control container-to-container communication and security isolation.
commands.example:
services:
  web:
    image: nginx
    networks:
      - frontend
  api:
    image: node-app
    networks:
      - frontend
      - backend

networks:
  frontend:
  backend:
commands.mistake: Failing to declare custom networks in the top-level 'networks:' block before referencing them within individual services.

commands.description: Defines sensitive data files (like keys, certificates, or passwords) that can be mounted into services securely without exposing them in environment variables.
commands.example:
services:
  app:
    image: my-secure-app
    secrets:
      - db_password

secrets:
  db_password:
    file: ./secrets/db_password.txt
commands.mistake: Committing the raw secret files to public Git repositories, or path mismatches causing Docker to fail to find the secret file.

commands.description: Defines non-sensitive configuration files (like nginx configs, app settings) at the top level, allowing services to mount them as read-only files.
commands.example:
services:
  web:
    image: nginx
    configs:
      - source: nginx_config
        target: /etc/nginx/nginx.conf

configs:
  nginx_config:
    file: ./config/nginx.conf
commands.mistake: Editing the source config file on the host and expecting immediate updates inside the container without restarting the service container.

commands.description: Specifies build options that are applied at image build time when running 'docker compose build' or starting services.
commands.example:
services:
  api:
    build:
      context: ./backend
      dockerfile: Dockerfile.dev
      args:
        NODE_ENV: development
commands.mistake: Using a build definition and expecting Docker Compose to automatically rebuild on every run. You must run 'docker compose up --build' to force updates.

commands.description: Specifies the path to the directory containing a Dockerfile, or a URL to a git repository, used as the build context.
commands.mistake: Setting context to a path containing large unused folders (like node_modules or large datasets) without a .dockerignore file, leading to slow build times.

commands.description: Specifies an alternate Dockerfile name relative to the build context directory. Default is 'Dockerfile'.
commands.mistake: Specifying an absolute path to the Dockerfile or a path that falls outside of the declared build context directory.

commands.description: Defines build-time arguments (ARG) passed to the Dockerfile. These are not accessible inside the running container.
commands.syntax:
build:
  args:
    - <key>=<value>
# Or key: value format
commands.mistake: Confusing 'build: args' (build-time variables) with 'environment' (runtime variables). Image containers cannot access build args after compilation.

commands.description: Builds the specified stage named in a multi-stage Dockerfile.
commands.example:
build:
  context: .
  dockerfile: Dockerfile
  target: builder
commands.mistake: Referencing a stage name that does not exist in the target Dockerfile, resulting in a build error.

commands.description: Specifies a list of images that the engine uses for cache resolution during image building.
commands.mistake: Using cached images that are stale, or failing to push built stages beforehand to allow other environments (like CI/CD) to pull cache.

commands.description: Specifies the image to start the container from. If the image does not exist locally, Compose attempts to pull it from the registry.
commands.mistake: Using the 'latest' tag in production. Image tags can be updated silently in registries, causing unpredictable deployments. Pin exact versions.

commands.description: Specifies a custom name for the container, rather than letting Docker Compose generate a default name based on project and service names.
commands.mistake: Defining a static container_name on a service that you plan to scale up. Custom names must be unique; running multiple instances will fail.

commands.description: Sets a custom hostname inside the container's shell environment.
commands.mistake: Confusing 'hostname' with network alias. Services resolve others by service name in Compose network, not hostname unless mapped.

commands.description: Adds metadata labels to the generated container, which are useful for documentation or monitoring tools like Traefik/Prometheus.
commands.mistake: Forgetting to quote labels with special characters (like dots or dashes), causing YAML parsing failures.

commands.description: Overrides the default user inside the container. You can specify user UID/GID or username.
commands.mistake: Running as a non-root UID while mounting volumes owned by root, leading to 'Permission Denied' errors inside the container.

commands.description: Overrides the default working directory specified in the Dockerfile (WORKDIR) for commands.
commands.mistake: Setting a working_dir that does not exist in the image or volume mounts, causing container execution crashes immediately.

commands.description: Defines runtime environment variables available inside the running container.
commands.mistake: Exposing raw passwords, secret keys, or API tokens directly in the compose file. Use env files or secrets instead.

commands.description: Loads environment variables from an external file (.env). Keeps sensitive keys out of docker-compose.yml.
commands.mistake: Forgetting to add .env files to .gitignore, resulting in secrets being pushed to source control repos.

commands.description: Overrides the default command (CMD) specified in the container image.
commands.example:
command: python manage.py runserver 0.0.0.0:8000
# Or array:
command: ["npm", "run", "dev"]
commands.mistake: Passing commands that exit immediately. Docker containers exit when their primary command completes. Use persistent loops or daemons.

commands.description: Overrides the default entrypoint specified in the container image.
commands.mistake: Replacing entrypoint completely without maintaining scripts that handle initializations like database migrations or signal forwarding.

commands.description: Gives the container root-level access to the host kernel features and devices. Equivalent to running commands directly on host.
commands.mistake: Using 'privileged: true' casually. It creates major security risks; if the container is compromised, the host can be taken over.

commands.description: Exposes container ports to the host machine. Defines mapping as host:container.
commands.mistake: Binding public ports on all interfaces by default (e.g. '5432:5432'), which exposes internal databases to the public internet.

commands.description: Declares internal ports that are accessible ONLY by linked services on the same Docker network. They are not exposed to the host system.
commands.mistake: Using 'expose' and trying to access the service from the host browser. Expose is strictly for container-to-container communication.

commands.description: Specifies custom networks that the service containers connect to, mapping defined network aliases.
commands.mistake: Connecting services to different custom networks and expecting them to resolve each other's domain names. They must share at least one network.

commands.description: Adds hostname mappings to the container's internal /etc/hosts file. Useful for resolving host domains.
commands.mistake: Hardcoding direct IP addresses that fluctuate in DHCP environments, breaking connection paths.

commands.description: Defines custom DNS servers inside the container to override the default system or host DNS resolvers.
commands.mistake: Specifying internal DNS servers that the host network cannot resolve, cutting off container external internet access.

commands.description: Mounts host paths (bind mounts) or named volumes into the service container at a designated directory path.
commands.mistake: Using relative host paths (like './data') and forgetting that paths are evaluated relative to the directory containing the docker-compose.yml file.

commands.description: Mounts a temporary in-memory directory inside the container, speeding up disk writes and saving flash drive wear.
commands.mistake: Storing persistent data in tmpfs mounts. All data is completely lost when the container stops or restarts.

commands.description: Expresses startup and shutdown dependencies between services, ensuring dependencies start first.
commands.example:
depends_on:
  db:
    condition: service_healthy
  redis:
    condition: service_started
commands.mistake: Assuming depends_on waits for a service to be fully ready before starting. You must use 'condition: service_healthy' alongside a declared healthcheck.

commands.description: Defines container restart policies when containers exit due to failures or engine restarts.
commands.mistake: Setting restart to 'always' on temporary utility containers, which creates infinite loops of container start/stop cycles.

commands.description: Declares a test configuration run periodically to check if the service container is healthy.
commands.example:
healthcheck:
  test: ["CMD", "curl", "-f", "http://localhost:8080/health"]
  interval: 30s
  timeout: 10s
  retries: 3
  start_period: 40s
commands.mistake: Setting a short timeout or omitting curl/ping utilities inside slim/alpine images, causing health checks to fail constantly.

commands.description: Runs an init process (tini) inside the container as PID 1 to forward signals and reap zombie processes.
commands.mistake: Omitting 'init: true' when running JVM, Node.js, or Python apps that spawn child processes, leading to container memory leaks and failure to terminate on 'Ctrl+C'.

commands.description: Defines deployment configurations for Swarm mode or Compose V2 (such as scaling, resources, and updates).
commands.example:
deploy:
  replicas: 3
  resources:
    limits:
      cpus: '0.50'
      memory: 512M
commands.mistake: Adding deploy properties and expecting them to enforce resources in legacy docker-compose v1 without using newer engine commands.

commands.description: Specifies the number of container instances running simultaneously for a service.
commands.mistake: Scaling services that bind to a specific static host port. Only one container can bind to a port; others will crash.

commands.description: Sets physical CPU and memory limits/reservations to restrict container resource consumption on the host system.
commands.syntax:
deploy:
  resources:
    limits:
      cpus: '<limit>'
      memory: <limit>
    reservations:
      cpus: '<reserved>'
      memory: <reserved>
commands.example:
deploy:
  resources:
    limits:
      cpus: '0.50'
      memory: 256M
    reservations:
      cpus: '0.25'
      memory: 128M
commands.mistake: Setting memory limits too low for the application runtime (e.g. Node/Java), resulting in OOMKilled (Out of Memory) crash loops.

commands.description: Configures how containers should be restarted when deploy specifications are active (replaces standard restart key in Swarm/V2).
commands.syntax:
deploy:
  restart_policy:
    condition: on-failure | any | none
    delay: <time>
    max_attempts: <count>
    window: <time>
commands.example:
deploy:
  restart_policy:
    condition: on-failure
    delay: 5s
    max_attempts: 3
    window: 120s
commands.mistake: Mixing the top-level 'restart' key with 'deploy: restart_policy' inside Swarm. Swarm ignores the standard service restart parameter.

commands.description: Builds, (re)creates, starts, and attaches to containers for all or specified services. Runs in foreground by default.
commands.example:
docker compose up -d
docker compose up --build web-api
commands.mistake: Forgetting the '-d' flag when running in terminal, which blocks the shell and displays logs until terminated, or neglecting to use '--build' when Dockerfile code changes.

commands.description: Stops containers and removes containers, networks, volumes, and images created by 'up'. Clean up resources.
commands.example:
docker compose down
docker compose down -v --rmi all
commands.mistake: Forgetting that 'docker compose down' by default does not delete named volumes. Add '-v' or '--volumes' if you need a clean state.

commands.description: Build or rebuild images associated with services defined in the Compose file. Does not start containers.
commands.mistake: Assuming 'docker compose build' automatically runs the new container. You must run 'docker compose up' afterwards to instantiate it.

commands.description: List all containers associated with the current Compose project, displaying their command, state, and port mappings.
commands.mistake: Failing to see a stopped or crashed container because you ran 'docker compose ps' without the '--all' flag in older versions.

commands.description: View aggregated log output from all running services or a specific service container.
commands.mistake: Running logs without '-f' (follow) or '--tail' limits on large projects, resulting in terminal spam and long buffer delays.

commands.description: Run an interactive command inside a running service container. Most commonly used to spawn an interactive shell.
commands.example:
docker compose exec -it db psql -U postgres
docker compose exec api sh
commands.mistake: Trying to run 'exec' on a container/service that is not currently running. To start a container and run a command, use 'docker compose run'.

commands.description: Run a one-off command against a service. Spawns a new temporary container with the service's configuration.
commands.example: docker compose run --rm api python manage.py migrate
commands.mistake: Running commands without the '--rm' flag, which leaves zombie stopped containers behind on the host system.

commands.description: Fetch and download the latest tag versions of registry images declared in the services section.
commands.mistake: Deploying updates without running pull first, which might cause compose to continue using older cached image versions on the host.

commands.description: Validate syntax, parse environment variables, and print the merged, expanded docker-compose configuration.
commands.mistake: Deploying Compose files to production without checking the resolved configuration via 'config' first to confirm environment interpolations.

commands.description: Restart all running service containers or specific ones. Keeps network and volume states intact.
commands.mistake: Using 'restart' to apply environment or docker-compose.yml configuration changes. Compose configuration updates require 'docker compose up -d'.
